title: Cancellation, timeout and stale results

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific JavaScript: Language and Browser Applications draft for Cancellation, timeout and stale results; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

canonicalExplanation.summary: AbortController communicates cancellation to APIs such as fetch, and consumers distinguish intentional abort from other failures.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A new search aborts the previous fetch and ignores its AbortError while reporting real network failures. Your goal is to implement and test cancellation or latest-request-wins behavior for an interactive fetch. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Call stack and event loop model; Closures and lifetime; Fetch and HTTP responses; Event propagation and delegation. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: AbortController communicates cancellation to APIs such as fetch, and consumers distinguish intentional abort from other failures. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Abort is cooperative, not every operation supports the signal, and cancellation does not roll back completed side effects. This draft therefore rejects: Dropping a promise variable automatically cancels its underlying request.

includes: Abort obsolete work and prevent older responses from overwriting current interface state.
Implement and test cancellation or latest-request-wins behavior for an interactive fetch.
excludes: Abort is cooperative, not every operation supports the signal, and cancellation does not roll back completed side effects.

outcomes: Implement and test cancellation or latest-request-wins behavior for an interactive fetch.

examples.title: Cancellation, timeout and stale results: concrete case
examples.situation: A new search aborts the previous fetch and ignores its AbortError while reporting real network failures.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: A new search aborts the previous fetch and ignores its AbortError while reporting real network failures.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Promises do not cancel by being ignored; pass and trigger an AbortSignal to supported operations.
examples.result: The result follows because Promises do not cancel by being ignored; pass and trigger an AbortSignal to supported operations.
